retrieved_docs.title: Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy is a genetically acquired disease that leads to progressive muscle weakness and severely decreased functional capacity in affected individuals—the understanding of how the interplay of epigenetic factors has evolved substantially in recent years. This activity reviews the current knowledge of the etiology and epidemiology of facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy. This activity outlines the evaluation and treatment of facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy and explains the role of the interprofessional team in improving the care of patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: For individuals with facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy, shoulder surgery might be beneficial to stabilize the shoulder. [202] When scoliotic curves exceed 20°, spinal correction through surgery can prolong respiratory function, walking ability, or both. [118]

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy
retrieved_docs.content: Genetics: Autosomal dominant disorder, involving DUX4 gene. Clinical features: Progressive weakness in face, shoulders, and upper arms. Diagnostic tests: Genetic testing for DUX4 gene mutations. Prognosis: Variable, may involve asymmetric muscle weakness.

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmaceutical Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: Steroids, specifically glucocorticoids like prednisone, administered at a dose of 0.75 mg/kg per day, significantly slow the progression of muscular dystrophy for up to 3 years. [189] While some patients may not tolerate glucocorticoid therapy due to factors like weight gain and an increased risk of fractures, recent evidence suggests that early oral steroid use can lead to markedly improved outcomes. [190] [191] This includes additional walking years for children and increased life expectancy. [71] The use of glucocorticoids in Becker dystrophy and their effectiveness in treating myotonic muscular dystrophy remain areas where research is ongoing, and evidence is not yet conclusive. [192] [193] Some individuals with facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy improve with steroid therapy, particularly when rapidly progressive weakness is observed. [194]

retrieved_docs.title: Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There are currently no disease-modifying treatments for FSHD. To this end, the mainstay of management of FSHD is supportive care. This care primarily occurs in the context of physical therapy and rehabilitation exercises. A 2016 clinical trial of home exercise therapy investigated the utility of combined aerobic and strength training programs in FSHD patients. The results demonstrated that individuals engaged in a 6-month program lead to statistically significant improvements in muscle function without further damaging diseased tissue. [11] In cases of severe pathology, physical therapy alone may not be enough to correct functional limitations. Assistive devices are, therefore, useful and can be tailored to the specific needs of each patient. Foot drop can be partially corrected with the use of ankle-foot-orthoses or in combination with knee-ankle-foot orthoses. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis in FSHD revolves around the quality of life in an affected individual. While the overall lifespan in these patients is not affected, roughly 20 percent of affected individuals will experience disability significant enough to require wheelchair use during the course of the disease. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Severe scapular winging can be corrected through scapular fixation via orthopedic surgery. While no randomized clinical trials have taken place examining the efficacy of this intervention, case reports have demonstrated both an improvement in shoulder abduction, pain, and activities of daily living. A more thorough investigation is therefore required to elucidate the utility of scapular fixation in the routine management of FSHD. [13] Managing pain and fatigue in FSHD patients is essential as both of these symptoms can affect patient psychology. Chronic pain is manageable with analgesic and antidepressant therapy to improve the quality of life in FSHD patients. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- History and Physical -- Pseudohypertrophy
retrieved_docs.content: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy begins with weakness and atrophy of facial and shoulder girdle (scapulohumeral) muscles. [117] Limb-girdle muscular dystrophy diagnosis is confirmed by excluding acute events causing proximal weakness, and the clinical picture, including genetic pattern, excludes Duchenne and facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy. [117]

retrieved_docs.title: Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: EMG studies are generally characterized by low amplitude, polyphasic potentials present for brief time intervals. [7] MRI studies classically reveal normal muscle tissue interspersed with abnormal tissue. These MRI abnormalities are reflective of healthy muscle tissue being replaced by areas of fat and fibrous tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Facioscapulohumeral Muscular Dystrophy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (FSHD) is a genetic illness inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion that affects skeletal muscle tissue in affected individuals. Muscle groups involved include those of the face, shoulder girdle, and lower extremity affected asymmetrically. [1] [2] FSHD characteristically starts proximally in the face and spreads distally to affected muscle groups. Beyond musculature, 50% of FSHD patients also present with subclinical high-frequency hearing loss and retinovasculopathy. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Muscular Dystrophy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy is another type of muscular dystrophy that affects approximately 1 in every 20,000 individuals worldwide. Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy typically presents with a later onset and slower progression than other types of muscular dystrophy, with symptoms often emerging in the teenage or adult years. This condition is caused by mutations in the DUX4 gene, which results in inappropriate expression of a toxic protein in muscle cells.

retrieved_docs.content: Scapuloperoneal muscular dystrophy, a variant of facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy, is different in that distal muscles in the lower extremity are involved early rather than the early sign of facial and shoulder muscle weakness seen in facioscapulohumeral dystrophy. retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serves as a parent category that includes both nedokromil and nusinersen. Nedokromil is identified as a member of the class of antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excluding corticosteroids, indicating its use in respiratory conditions where allergy‑mediated bronchoconstriction is a concern. Nusinersen, while also belonging to the broader N [Preparations] group, is a distinct therapeutic agent used for the treatment of spinal muscular atrophy, reflecting a neuromuscular application rather than a respiratory one. This organization demonstrates that the N [Preparations] category encompasses a range of medications with different mechanisms and clinical indications, from inhaled antiallergic agents like nedokromil to antisense oligonucleotide therapies such as nusinersen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 569
retrieved_docs.content: Aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen, and nusinersen are all classified within the ATC group “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” indicating that each is used to address conditions affecting the musculo‑skeletal system. Nusinersen’s therapeutic action is characterized by an increase in protein synthesis, a physiologic effect that contributes to its clinical benefit in these disorders. The shared classification of these three agents underscores their common relevance to musculo‑skeletal disease management, while the distinct mechanism of increased protein synthesis by nusinersen highlights the importance of understanding individual drug actions for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 368
retrieved_docs.content: The category N [Preparations] serves as a parent grouping for several distinct agents, including the antiallergic inhalant nedokromil and the antisense oligonucleotide therapy nusinersen. Nedokromil is classified as an antiallergic inhalant for obstructive airway diseases (excluding corticosteroids) and is chemically a member of the Carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing product class. Tilidin and mupirocin are also members of this same carboxylic‑acid‑derivative class, indicating a shared structural foundation despite their different therapeutic indications. Both mupirocin (a topical antibiotic) and nusinersen (a treatment for spinal muscular atrophy) produce the physiologic effect of Increased Protein Synthesis, illustrating that agents with diverse clinical uses can converge on similar biological outcomes. Recognizing these chemical and functional relationships helps clinicians understand drug classification, anticipate potential cross‑effects, and optimize patient‑centered care.
